q.value: What is the derivative of the unit step function u(t)?

questions.explanation: (B) The derivative of unit step is the Dirac delta (impulse).

q.value:
The function f(t) has the Fourier Trasnsform G(w).
The Fourier transform of G(t) i.e. G(t) = $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} G(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt$ is:

explanation.value: (C) Using the duality property of the Fourier Transform: if $$f(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} g(\omega)$$, then $$g(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi f(-\omega)$$. Hence, the Fourier transform of $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt$$ is $$2\pi f(-\omega)$$.

q.value:
Match the following signal types:

List-I (Signal)List-II (Property)
Continuous-time periodic signal(P) Periodic
Continuous-time non-periodic signal(Q) Non-periodic
Discrete-time periodic signal(P) Periodic
Discrete-time non-periodic signal(Q) Non-periodic

Choose the correct option:

explanation.value: (A)The classification of signals into periodic and non-periodic is independent of whether the signal is continuous-time or discrete-time. Both continuous-time and discrete-time signals can be periodic as well as non-periodic. Hence, all given matches are correct.

q.value:
Match the following transforms:

List-I (Transform)List-II (Signal Type)
DTFS(P) Discrete-time periodic
DTFT(Q) Discrete-time non-periodic
CTFS(R) Continuous-time periodic
CTFT(S) Continuous-time non-periodic

Choose the correct option:

options: Only DTFS and CTFS are correct
Only DTFT and CTFT are correct

explanation.value: (A) Fourier Series (DTFS, CTFS) is used for periodic signals, while Fourier Transform (DTFT, CTFT) is used for non-periodic signals. DT indicates discrete-time signals and CT indicates continuous-time signals. Hence, all given matches are correct.

q.value:
The signals $f_1(t)$ and $f_2(t)$ are shown below. The convolution of $f_1(t)$ and $f_2(t)$, i.e
y(t) = $f_1(t)$ * $f_2(t)$$ will be represanted by which of the following option given below:

explanation.value:
Convolution of two signals:
  1. Limits of convolved signal:
      - Upper limit = sum of upper limits of individual signals.
      - Lower limit = sum of lower limits of individual signals.
  2. Duration:
      - Duration of convolved signal = duration of signal 1 + duration of signal 2.
  3. Shape:
      - Convolution slides one signal over another, multiplying and summing overlaps.
  4. Linearity & Commutativity:
       x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t).
  5. Energy spread:
      - Energy spreads over combined interval of two signals.
  6. Impulse property:
      - Convolution with delta function reproduces the other signal.
